title: Effect of Information on Asynchronous Radio Transmission Complexity
author: Ayeoribe, Olarewaju Peter
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: After analyzing asynchronous deterministic broadcasting in radio networks, the results indicate that this method can greatly improve communication efficiency and decrease latency. Future studies may investigate its potential for real-time data transmission across different network setups. This method facilitates effective communication among nodes, reducing latency and optimizing throughput. By utilizing advanced algorithms, we can improve the reliability of data transmission even under difficult conditions. The implementation of a broadcasting protocol involves a series of events, each characterized by the simultaneous transmission or delivery of messages. The goal is to guarantee that the source message is received by all nodes within the network. When two messages are sent to a node at the same time, a collision occurs, preventing that node from receiving any information. An asynchronous adversary can delay message deliveries, leading to unintended collisions and hindering the spread of messages. The total number of message transmissions executed by a protocol in the worst-case scenario is known as the work of the protocol, which serves as an indicator of its complexity. This paper seeks to explore how different types of information available to nodes influence the optimal work of an asynchronous broadcasting protocol. This information may relate to previous events that could affect node behaviour (adaptive versus oblivious protocols), or it may concern the network's topology or specific parameters. We illustrate that limiting the knowledge accessible to nodes can result in an exponential increase in the work required by an asynchronous broadcasting protocol, and in some cases, it may even make broadcasting unfeasible. The aim of this research is to analyze how various types of information that nodes can access affect the optimal functioning of an asynchronous broadcasting protocol. This information may pertain to the network's structure.
